title: Ecosystem Components Interactive Explorer
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive p5.js drag-and-sort activity where students classify 16 ecosystem components as biotic or abiotic factors and explore their interactions.

learningObjectives: Classify ecosystem components as biotic or abiotic factors
Explain the difference between biotic and abiotic factors with specific examples
Describe how biotic and abiotic factors interact within an ecosystem
Identify components that may be challenging to classify and explain why
prerequisites: Basic understanding of living vs. nonliving things
Introduction to the concept of an ecosystem
Familiarity with common organisms and environmental factors

keyQuestions: What makes something a biotic factor vs. an abiotic factor?
Are dead leaves biotic or abiotic? What about soil?
How do biotic and abiotic factors depend on each other?
Can an ecosystem exist with only biotic or only abiotic factors?
commonMisconceptions: Dead organisms are abiotic because they are no longer alive
Soil is purely abiotic (it actually contains both biotic and abiotic components)
Biotic and abiotic factors operate independently of each other
assessmentOpportunities: Sorting accuracy score as formative assessment
Written explanations for commonly misclassified items
Biotic-abiotic interaction descriptions after completing the sort
